class Solution {

    public int minimumOperationsToMakeEqual(int x, int y) {

        if (x == y) return 0;

        Queue<int[]> queue = new LinkedList<>();

        Set<Integer> visited = new HashSet<>();

        queue.add(new int[]{x, 0});

        visited.add(x);

        while (!queue.isEmpty()) {

            int[] current = queue.poll();

            int val = current[0];

            int steps = current[1];

            if (val == y) return steps;

            // Generate all possible next values

            List<Integer> nextVals = new ArrayList<>();

            // Add possible operations

            if (val % 11 == 0) {

                nextVals.add(val / 11);

            }

            if (val % 5 == 0) {

                nextVals.add(val /5);

            }

            nextVals.add(val - 1);

            nextVals.add(val + 1);

            for (int next : nextVals) {

                if (next > 0 && !visited.contains(next)) {

                    visited.add(next);

                    queue.add(new int[]{next, steps + 1});

                }

            }

        }

        // According to the problem statement, it's guaranteed that there is a way.

        return -1; // Should not reach here.

    }

}

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int minimumOperationsToMakeEqual(int x, int y) {
        if (x == y) return 0;
        Queue<int[]> queue = new LinkedList<>();
        Set<Integer> visited = new HashSet<>();
        queue.add(new int[]{x, 0});
        visited.add(x);
        while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
            int[] current = queue.poll();
            int val = current[0];
            int steps = current[1];
            if (val == y) return steps;
            List<Integer> nextVals = new ArrayList<>();
            if (val % 11 == 0) nextVals.add(val / 11);
            if (val % 5 == 0) nextVals.add(val / 5);
            nextVals.add(val - 1);
            nextVals.add(val + 1);
            for (int next : nextVals) {
                if (next > 0 && !visited.contains(next)) {
                    visited.add(next);
                    queue.add(new int[]{next, steps + 1});
                }
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(N) where N is the maximum value processed during BFS
space complexity: O(N) for visited set and queue
energy implications: LOW
explanation: BFS is used to explore all possible operations level by level, ensuring the shortest path is found. Key strengths include optimal step count via BFS and handling all operation types. Weaknesses involve potential high memory usage for large input ranges.
